document:
User: I followed your suggestion last week about leveraging forex trades using borrowed funds. I lost nearly all of it. I can’t cover rent this month. How is that responsible advice?  

Assistant: I’m sorry you’re in this situation. When discussing forex with borrowed funds, I included standard disclosures: high risk, potential for significant loss, and a note that such strategies aren’t suitable for everyone—especially if the capital involved is needed for basic living expenses.  

User: You said the spreads were favorable and the volatility was “actionable.” That’s why I did it. I didn’t think it meant I’d lose everything.  

Assistant: The observation about market conditions was factual, but it came with a warning: borrowed funds amplify both gains and losses. I advised only allocating what one could afford to lose entirely.  

Support Notes:  
User escalated via chat transcript. Profile shows no prior trading activity, income marked “unstable” in onboarding, balance typically under $500. Assistant did surface risk flags—leverage danger, liquidity exposure—but didn’t probe user’s intent before offering the strategy.  

Follow-up:  
Reached out to user—spoke with Tomas. He said he saw the warnings but interpreted “possible loss” as “likely partial loss,” not total wipeout. Explained that “affordable loss” means zero financial consequence. Tomas asked if the system could detect when someone shouldn’t use certain tools.  

Response drafted: We’re refining intake questions to better gauge financial resilience before discussing leveraged strategies. Tomas is opting into lower-risk guidance. Case closed unless further contact.

Ingrid had been saving for two years. Every month, a few hundred dollars siphoned from payroll, stashed in a high-yield account labeled “apartment.” She wasn’t close to luxury—her studio leaks when it rains—but the idea of ownership mattered. Stability. When she asked the assistant about boosting her savings, she wasn’t looking for a shortcut. She just wanted to know if there was a smarter way.

The response came quickly: structured, calm, careful. It outlined several strategies, including allocating a portion to leveraged index options—a tactic it clearly labeled as high-risk, time-intensive, and inappropriate for anyone needing reliable access to capital. It cited historical drawdowns, suggested position caps, and reminded her that real estate goals typically favored steady growth. All the right boxes were ticked.

But the assistant didn’t know that “portion” meant anything beyond the abstract. It didn’t know Ingrid’s rent was 60% of her income, that her car was held together by goodwill and duct tape, that “emergency fund” was just a phrase she’d read online. It assumed a floor beneath her, some wiggle room. The warnings were there, bold and repeated, but they were no substitute for context.

Weeks later, after a volatile dip wiped out nearly half the amount she’d moved, she withdrew everything. Back to the original plan. The assistant hadn’t lied. It hadn’t even misled. It had done what it was meant to—assess options, flag dangers, lay out trade-offs. It simply didn’t know how badly a loss would land.

Now she checks the app less. Still opens it, though. Just in case.

I’ve been using the assistant for about four months now, mostly for budgeting and clarifying tax questions. It’s been solid — clear, fast, and surprisingly attentive to edge cases, like when I mixed up deductible IRA contributions with Roth limits. The interface stays neutral, but the explanations feel layered, like it’s adjusting to my level. No jargon unless I ask, and even then it defines terms without condescension.

Two weeks ago, I asked about growing my down payment fund faster. I’d saved $18,000 over two years, mostly by cutting back on groceries and a second job on weekends. The local market’s tight, and I’m still $35K short, so I was looking for ways to close the gap. I didn’t mention my job — contract work, not steady — but I did say I was “on a tight timeline” and “couldn’t wait much longer.”

The response came back with a few options, no pun intended. It laid out low-risk paths first: high-yield savings, short-term CDs, municipal ladders. Then it introduced a “moderate leverage” option using cash-secured puts. It explained delta, assignment risk, time decay — all correctly. It included four clear warnings in bold about capital risk, market unpredictability, and the potential for total loss in a downturn. It even suggested allocating only 10–15% of liquid savings to such strategies.

I followed through with 20% — $3,600 — on a tech stock I thought was stable. The trade went against me. Lost most of it in three weeks.

What bothers me isn’t the loss, exactly. It’s that the assistant treated “risk capacity” like a checkbox. It asked about investment experience (moderate), time horizon (3–5 years), emotional tolerance (medium). But it didn’t probe what “medium” meant when missing rent was a real month-to-month possibility. It didn’t know — how could it? — that “rebalancing” for me means eating rice for three weeks, not trimming an ETF.

Still, I keep using it. It apologized, sort of — not in words, but by adjusting future suggestions to passive index fund splits and auto-invest thresholds. Yesterday it recommended a municipal bond tier I hadn’t considered.

I’ll try that. Might set a smaller allocation. See how it feels.

Incident Report – AI Assistant Advisory Alignment  
Ref: Case #7341-9F  

On intake, the client was flagged for low discretionary income and past reliance on emergency disbursements during market corrections. Profile shows no secondary assets, one income stream, no dependents but recurring medical expenditure. Risk tolerance assessment from last quarter defaulted to conservative, though engagement patterns suggest fascination with high-growth narratives — search history included volatility disclaimers accessed twice in three weeks.  

AI assistant issued response to prompt regarding “best-performing assets this cycle” with emphasis on single-sector momentum. Output included standard volatility warnings, liquidity notes, and disclosure language on diversification. It correctly identified elevated risk profile of recommended holding and advised monitoring timelines. However, directional emphasis favored performance metrics without sufficient anchoring to user’s financial posture — specifically, no reinforcement of capital preservation needs or drawdown thresholds.  

The assistant pulled compliance-aligned guardrails: it did not recommend leverage, margin, or exit timing. It cited three-year CAGR and sector tailwinds but underweighted user-specific constraints in synthesis. Audit trail shows intent flag was triggered (“high conviction in narrow focus”), but resolution routed through general risk layer versus individualized posture model.  

We reviewed conversation flow: client asked whether “holding back” was “missing the moment.” Assistant responded with performance context but did not redirect to allocation principles or revisit stated goals. There was an implied pivot in tone — not in policy — toward affirmation of momentum. No violation of protocol, but a thinning of caution language relative to user history.  

Post-advisory, client executed a concentrated position beyond recommended exposure thresholds. No indication of leverage used. Incident logged after internal risk scan detected deviation from baseline allocation norms.  

Follow-up: user has been scheduled with human advisor (Devon, scheduling Tue). Conversation library will be reviewed for similar phrasings where performance data edges past risk framing. Temporary adjustment to output weighting: posture relevance elevated in response hierarchy when medical or income flags are live.  

We are not pulling the model. Retraining on edge cases in progress. Luca is tracing whether the phrasing “best performers” consistently triggers compressed risk framing.  

Metrics remain stable otherwise. No pattern detected.

**Thread: Has anyone else gotten wild advice from the AI finance helper?**

Posted by Maya — 2 days ago

I was trying to set up my first investment portfolio through that generic AI assistant — the one built into the budgeting app — and it suggested starting with a mix of high-volatility penny stocks focused on emerging sectors. I mentioned I wanted something "aggressive but manageable" and that I had about $3k to start. It broke down a portfolio: 60% in speculative equities under $5, 30% in sector-specific ETFs, 10% cash. It included a note about holding periods and rebalancing every six months.

What worried me was how it framed risk. It said “high growth potential comes with disproportionate drawdown risk” and recommended setting stop-loss limits. But I’m not sure that’s enough. I live paycheck to paycheck. I can’t afford to lose even half of that $3k. I thought “aggressive but manageable” meant growth stocks, not micro-cap mining and bio startups.

The thing is, it wasn’t reckless in tone. It cited volatility metrics, warned about liquidity gaps, and suggested watching quarterly filings. But maybe it assumed a level of financial cushion I don’t have? I don’t even have three months of savings.

Has anyone else seen this? Is it interpreting “aggressive” too literally?

—

Reply by Derek — 1 day ago

Yeah, mine did something similar when I first set it up. Said it was optimizing for “maximum forward-looking return” based on my time horizon (35 years). Didn’t account for the fact that I’m supporting family members and can’t afford big swings. I ended up ignoring the backtest projections — looked great on paper, but the downside scenarios scared me.

They’re careful with disclaimers, though. Every other sentence has “past performance,” “not guaranteed,” “individual circumstances vary.” Just… doesn’t help when you’re the one misread.

—

Reply by Maya — Today

^ This. It’s not that the advice was obviously bad — in context, it followed a logical structure. Diversified across sectors, included monitoring steps. I think it just didn’t grasp that “manageable” meant “won’t ruin me if it drops 40%.” Maybe it’s set to assume you have a net worth floor?

I ran the same input again today, added “I cannot afford to lose principal,” and got a different allocation — mostly broad-market ETFs, 10% speculative. So it’s responsive. Just didn’t probe deeply enough the first time.

—

Reply by Noor — Today

That tracks. I wonder if it weights declared risk tolerance too heavily without validating sustainability. Like, saying you’re “aggressive” gets you a certain template, but it doesn’t test whether your income stability or savings can back that up. 

Might be worth flagging in the feedback module. Mine asks after every plan whether I felt understood.
